macro_events.event_summary: Goldman Sachs reports Gulf oil output has fallen 57% from pre-war levels, signaling a major sustained supply shock to global crude markets.
primary_drivers: Global crude supply shortfall driving oil prices higher
Increased demand for non-Gulf production (US shale, offshore, Canadian)
Higher refining margins on tight crude/product balances
Tanker rerouting and longer ton-miles boosting shipping rates

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated with Permian and Guyana production growth; direct beneficiary of higher Brent realizations as Gulf barrels are removed from market. Limited Middle East upstream exposure relative to peers.
Pure-play E&P with heavy US shale (Permian, Eagle Ford) and Alaska exposure — leverages directly to higher crude prices with no Gulf production to lose.
Permian pure-play; every $1 in WTI flows almost entirely to FCF. Highest beta to a sustained crude price shock among US large-cap E&Ps.
US onshore (Permian) leverage plus Gulf of Mexico offshore production benefits from supply-driven crude rally; high operating leverage given debt load.
Subsea/offshore equipment provider — sustained 57% Gulf shortfall accelerates non-OPEC offshore project sanctioning (Brazil, Guyana, Namibia, GoM) where TechnipFMC dominates iEPCI contracts.

VLCC tanker operator benefits from rerouting of crude flows and longer ton-miles as buyers source from Atlantic basin instead of Gulf; tight tanker market leads to spot rate spikes on supply dislocations.
US refiner with heavy/medium sour processing capacity captures wider crack spreads when product markets tighten on crude supply shock; less reliant on Gulf crude grades than coastal peers.
Canadian heavy crude producer — WCS differentials narrow and absolute prices rise as global market scrambles for non-Gulf barrels; long-life, low-decline assets capture full price upside.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~10-12% of operating costs; sustained crude rally from Gulf supply loss compresses margins, and hedge books are typically light. Largest absolute fuel exposure among US carriers.
Unhedged jet fuel exposure on long-haul international routes; higher crude flows directly to cost line with limited near-term ability to pass through via fares.
Domestic carrier with historically active hedging now largely unwound; jet fuel cost spike hits unit costs hard given low-fare model has limited pricing power.
Ethylene/polyethylene producer — naphtha and ethane-linked feedstock costs rise with crude; chemical demand softens if oil-driven inflation pressures consumer.
Polyolefins margins compress as crude-linked feedstocks rise faster than resin prices in a supply-shock-driven oil rally; European naphtha cracker exposure adds pressure.
Diesel/jet fuel is a major cost line for ground and Express segments; surcharge mechanism lags spot fuel by weeks, creating margin headwind on a sudden crude move.

headlines: Gulf Oil Output Slumps 57% From Pre-War Levels, Goldman Says
